g.text: Nested Loop JOIN — SELECT * FROM users u JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
Result Set — k combined rows (identical output, radically different cost)
Strategy A — Full Inner Scan O(n × m)
Strategy B — Index Inner Lookup O(n log m + k)
② Inner Cursor: orders — RESETS EVERY ITERATION
③ Evaluate Join Condition: u.id = o.user_id ?
Inner rewinds: n full table scans of orders
Pages read: n x pages(orders) — catastrophic
Complexity: O(n x m) <- BAD for large tables
① Outer Cursor: users (same outer loop)
Index probes: n x log2(m) — e.g. 13,000 ops
Table lookups: k total (only matching orders)
Pages read: O(n x index_depth + k) — efficient
Complexity: O(n log m + k) <- GOOD
OP_REWIND cursor_u // seek to first user

text.tspan: // Iterates n times total (one per user row)
// Iterates n times total (one per user row)

g.text: OP_REWIND cursor_o // EXPENSIVE: re-reads all m rows

text.tspan: // Repeated n times: n x m total row reads
// Repeated n times: n x m total row reads
// Same outer scan: n iterations — identical cost
// Same outer scan: n iterations — identical cost
// B+tree descent: O(log m) — 3-4 page reads only
// B+tree descent: O(log m) — 3-4 page reads only

g.text: emit(u JOIN o) // only k matched rows!
